figures.title: Minimizing Benchmark-Relative Drawdown Duration via Occupation Time Penalization
figures.caption: Figure 1 plots the interquartile range, median and mean of underwater curves from the Monte Carlo simulation. Note that the median dominates the mean, which is due to the occurrence of prolonged and deep drawdowns. For the observed time period and random seed, the 25th percentile of paths does not breach the critical drawdown level. — This figure displays benchmark-relative drawdown duration over a 24-year horizon, comparing an optimal drawdown-duration strategy against a buy-and-hold benchmark

figures.title: Minimizing Benchmark-Relative Drawdown Duration via Occupation Time Penalization
figures.caption: Figure 1: Underwater curves with interquartile ranges — This line chart displays average benchmark-relative underwater curves across 5000 simulated paths over a 25-year investment horizon, with the y-axis showing the percentage underwater drawdown relative to a benchmark (ranging from 0% to -35%) and the x-axis showing time in years. figures.title: Strategic OTC market making with reputation feedback
figures.caption: Figure 1 displays the marginal continuation value of a successful event in each tier. For tier A, this is the continuation value gain of winning rather than losing an RFQ event. The effect is concentrated around the RFQ gate because a single win matters most when the dealer is close to a promotional threshold. figures.title: Strategic OTC market making with reputation feedback
figures.caption: Figure 2 shows how the RFQ tier reacts to reputation. The bid offset falls near the gate midpoint, which is the signature of a campaign: the dealer sacrifices immediate spread to improve the probability of winning and to protect future access. Above the gate, spreads widens as the dealer monetizes the increased flow, although the policy remains defensive because falling back through the gate is co — This figure displays two heatmaps showing promotion values across a two-dimensional score space w

figures.title: Strategic OTC market making with reputation feedback
figures.caption: Figure 3 summarizes the streaming response. When RB is low, the dealer tightens quotes to the spread floor in order to create more streaming requests and hence more chances to repair the fill-ratio score in physical time. The acceptance probability is nevertheless not pushed mechanically to one: the dealer still controls the last-look threshold and retains protection against adverse selection. figures.title: Reinforcement Learning for Execution under Dynamic Fees in a Closed-Loop DEX Simulator
figures.caption: Figure 1: The closed-loop market. The execution agent trades a target order across two constant- product pools; the dynamic-fee closure reprices both pools from own-pool state, rival-pool state, and the oracle gap; noise traders route by effective cost; an arbitrageur closes profitable CEX– AMM gaps. figures.caption: Figure 2 reports the frozen final block: mean shortfall for TWAP, tuned lookahead, and the DQN under each intra-step ordering, and the paired DQN−lookahead edge with its interval. figures.title: Diachronic Sample Integration: Robust Tail-Risk Estimation with Generative Models
figures.caption: Figure 1: Conceptual illustration of DSI. (a) Single checkpoints prioritize bulk distributional fidelity, often truncating extreme tails due to optimization noise. (b) DSI aggregates heterogeneous checkpoints across a single trajectory, the resulting mixture averages checkpoint-specific tail fluctuations while any persistent trajectory bias remains as an irreducible floor. — This figure illustrates two probability distribution estimation approaches using density plots. figures.caption: Figure 2: Dependence preservation under realistic sampling budgets. (a) Sum of absolute differences between refer- ence and generated correlation matrices (multivariate depen- dence). (b) Sum of absolute differences between reference and generated autocorrelation coefficients (temporal depen- dence). Lower values indicate better agreement. figures.caption: Figure 3: Sensitivity of DSI to mixture size K and check- point stride M under a fixed budget (N = 1000). Average relative error (VaR and ES, α = 5%) is plotted against K for different strides. Checkpoint values on the x-axis are dis- played with uniform spacing. Error decreases rapidly with increasing K and plateaus for large mixtures.
